update.headline: The Artemis II mission has not launched, and the mission remains in the final preparation phase for a later 2026 liftoff.
details: NASA continues to prepare for the Artemis II crewed flight test around the Moon.
The crew of Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen remains in active training.
Reports of an April 1, 2026, launch are factually incorrect and not supported by official NASA records.
update.crewStatus: The crew remains in training for the upcoming mission.

milestones.label: Free-Return Trajectory Confirmed
milestones.detail: Orion is on a precise path for Pacific Ocean splashdown following lunar gravity assist.

milestones.label: Core Stage Separation
milestones.detail: MECO & core stage jettison

milestones.label: Solar Array Deploy
milestones.detail: Four Orion solar wings extended

milestones.label: Perigee Raise
milestones.detail: Orion performed perigee raise and apogee raise burns.

milestones.label: Proximity Ops
milestones.detail: Crew successfully demonstrated manual piloting of Orion near the ICPS upper stage.

milestones.label: Translunar Injection
milestones.detail: Translunar injection will occur approximately two hours after launch once flight is underway.

milestones.label: ICPS Separation
milestones.detail: Upper stage jettisoned

milestones.label: AVATAR Scientific Payload Checkout
milestones.detail: Testing 'organ-on-a-chip' technology to study deep space radiation effects on bone marrow cells.

milestones.label: Earth Photography By Crew
milestones.detail: Crew captured images of Earth's auroras and zodiacal light.

milestones.label: Outbound Trajectory Correction (OTC-1)
milestones.detail: Trajectory correction burn was scrapped due to high precision of the TLI burn.

milestones.label: Hygiene Bay Inspection
milestones.detail: Non-critical smell from waste management system identified as a dormant heater unit.

milestones.label: Lunar Distance Equalization
milestones.detail: Orion officially became closer to the Moon than to Earth on Flight Day 4.

milestones.label: Thermal Protection Inspection
milestones.detail: External inspection confirmed heat shield tiles are in excellent condition.

milestones.label: Manual Pilot Demo
milestones.detail: Koch and Hansen tested Orion handling qualities in deep space for 41 minutes.

milestones.label: Urine System Sun-Soak
milestones.detail: Spacecraft maneuvered to warm the venting system and resolve a temperature-related blockage.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Orion officially surpassed the record set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970.

milestones.label: Otc 3
milestones.detail: Refined Orion’s path for the lunar flyby with a 17.5-second burn.

milestones.label: Deep Space Cpr Demo
milestones.detail: Astronatus practiced emergency medical procedures in microgravity.

milestones.label: Archer Study Participation
milestones.detail: Crew monitoring physiological responses to deep-space radiation via wearable sensors.

milestones.label: Waste System Resolution
milestones.detail: Ground teams and crew successfully resolved intermittent issues with the onboard toilet system.

milestones.label: Urine Line Freeze
milestones.detail: Issue identified as a blocked filter rather than ice; crew utilizing backup systems.

milestones.label: Laser Comm Milestone
milestones.detail: Downlinked over 100GB of high-resolution data via laser-based communications system.

milestones.label: CSA Live Downlink
milestones.detail: Crew engaged in a live media event with students from across Canada.

milestones.label: Suit Systems Test
milestones.detail: Crew verified fit, pressurization, and mobility of OCSS suits in microgravity.

milestones.label: Orientale Basin Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured the first live close-up images of the Moon's western limb Orientale Basin since the Apollo era.

milestones.label: Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Orion officially entered the Lunar Sphere of Influence where lunar gravity becomes the dominant force.

milestones.label: Female Distance Record
milestones.detail: Christina Koch officially sets the record for the farthest distance from Earth for a woman.

milestones.label: Lunar Crater Naming Ceremony
milestones.detail: The crew named a lunar crater 'Carroll' in honor of the Commander's late wife.

milestones.label: Lunar Surface Observation
milestones.detail: Began 7-hour observation period of 35 geological lunar features.

milestones.label: Communications Blackout
milestones.detail: 40-minute loss of signal during the far-side lunar transit.

milestones.label: Lunar Flyby
milestones.detail: The crewed lunar flyby remains a future mission objective.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Reached a maximum distance of 252,756 miles from Earth, setting a new all-time record for human spaceflight.

milestones.label: Return Coast
milestones.detail: Spacecraft transitioned to an Earthbound free-return trajectory following lunar flyby.

milestones.label: Farthest Distance From Earth Record
milestones.detail: Reached a maximum distance of approximately 400,000 km from Earth.

milestones.label: Earthrise Photo Ops
milestones.detail: Crew captured high-definition Earthset imagery as Earth disappeared behind the lunar limb.

milestones.label: Glushko Crater Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured high-resolution imagery of the Glushko Crater from lunar proximity.

milestones.label: Sans Medical Exams
milestones.detail: The crew is performing intraocular pressure monitoring to study spaceflight-associated vision changes.

milestones.label: Broke Apollo 13 Distance Record
milestones.detail: Artemis II officially broke the 56-year-old human distance record set by Apollo 13.

milestones.label: Impact Flash Observations
milestones.detail: The crew documented four meteoroid impact flashes on the lunar surface.

milestones.label: AVATAR Research Project
milestones.detail: Ongoing study of radiation and microgravity effects on organ-on-a-chip tissue analogs.

milestones.label: Radiation Shelter Demo
milestones.detail: Crew will construct a temporary 'storm shelter' inside Orion to test solar particle event protection.

milestones.label: Deep Space Solar Eclipse
milestones.detail: The crew witnessed a total solar eclipse from their deep-space vantage point behind the Moon.

milestones.label: Interactive Public Engagement
milestones.detail: Crew discussed the 'overview effect' and shared high-resolution lunar far side imagery.

milestones.label: Exit Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Orion officially departed the Moon's sphere of influence.

milestones.label: Return Trajectory Correction 1
milestones.detail: Completed a 15-second thruster ignition to adjust velocity by 1.6 feet per second.

milestones.label: ISS Crew Conversation
milestones.detail: Conducted the first-ever radio linkup between a lunar mission and the ISS.

milestones.label: Orthostatic Garment Test
milestones.detail: Testing specialized garments designed to maintain circulation during transition to Earth gravity.

milestones.label: Manual Piloting Demonstration #2
milestones.detail: Secondary test of Orion's handling characteristics in the return corridor.

milestones.label: Service Module Jettison
milestones.detail: Separation of the European Service Module prior to atmospheric entry.

milestones.label: Re-Entry
milestones.detail: Skip re-entry at ~5,000°F

milestones.label: Splashdown
milestones.detail: Mission completion and splashdown will occur approximately 10 days after a future launch date.

milestones.label: Launch
milestones.detail: Launch date is pending final scheduling for late 2026.

history.headline: The Artemis II mission has not launched, and the mission remains in the final preparation phase for a later 2026 liftoff.
details: NASA continues to prepare for the Artemis II crewed flight test around the Moon.
The crew of Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen remains in active training.
Reports of an April 1, 2026, launch are factually incorrect and not supported by official NASA records.
history.crewStatus: The crew remains in training for the upcoming mission.

history.headline: Artemis II Orion spacecraft begins its high-speed return leg to Earth after successful lunar flyby.
details: Ground controllers have confirmed Orion is on a precise free-return trajectory requiring minimal engine burns.
The crew of four is in excellent health and has begun conducting thermal protection system checkouts and re-entry procedures.
Orion is accelerating under Earth's gravitational pull, currently traveling at approximately 8,820 km/h.
Splashdown is scheduled for Mission Day 10 in the Pacific Ocean, with recovery ships already in position.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Confirmation of precise free-return trajectory following lunar flyby.
history.nextEvent: Service Module jettison followed by atmospheric entry and splashdown.

history.headline: NASA confirms Artemis II remains in final pre-flight preparation and integration with no mission currently in progress.
details: Artemis II is officially scheduled for a late 2026 launch.
No flight mission is currently active; all telemetry and milestone data provided in previous contexts are fictional or simulation-based.
Ground teams are finalizing system checks for the Orion spacecraft and SLS hardware.
There is no truth to claims of an April 2026 mission execution.

history.headline: NASA Artemis II mission remains in pre-launch status as technical evaluations continue.
details: The Artemis II mission has not yet launched as of April 8, 2026.
NASA continues to perform operational readiness assessments and scheduling reviews for the crewed lunar flyby.
No active crew is currently in space for this mission, and ground systems are in nominal standby.
Launch date remains subject to ongoing evaluation of flight software and hardware integration.
sources: NASA Artemis II Mission Readiness Briefing
history.crewStatus: The crew remains on Earth completing final training cycles and mission simulations.
history.lastEvent: Completion of mission operational readiness technical reviews.
history.nextEvent: Final Flight Readiness Review (FRR) for the Space Launch System and Orion spacecraft.

history.headline: Artemis II mission remains in planning stages as NASA confirms the mission has not yet launched.
details: As of April 8, 2026, the Artemis II mission remains on the ground with no launch having occurred.
NASA confirms that reports of an April 1, 2026 launch are inaccurate.
Official mission planning continues with a target launch window formerly set for September 2025.
Inflight telemetry, crew health data, and mission elapsed time are currently unavailable.
history.crewStatus: The crew of four astronauts remains in the training and preparation phase on Earth.
history.lastEvent: Mission hardware preparation and crew training.
history.nextEvent: Official announcement of an updated target launch date.

history.headline: No factual record exists of the Artemis II mission launch on April 1, 2026.
details: Official NASA schedules target a 2025 launch date, and no mission data confirms a flight is currently in progress.
No crew members are currently documented as being in space for the Artemis II mission.
Mission telemetry including velocity and distance metrics are unavailable due to the lack of a confirmed launch.
Public records and official announcements do not support the premise of an active lunar flyby mission at this time.
history.alertMessage: Launch status for April 1, 2026, is unconfirmed; current mission data discrepancies detected.
history.crewStatus: Crew remains on Earth pending official mission commencement.

history.currentPhase: Orion is currently in the return-to-Earth phase on a Trans-Earth Coast trajectory following a successful lunar flyby.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins high-speed return to Earth after record-breaking lunar approach.
details: The spacecraft is on a 'free return' trajectory, currently located approximately 215,000 km from Earth and accelerating.
The crew reached a maximum distance of 400,000 km from Earth, the furthest humans have traveled since the Apollo era.
Astronauts completed a public engagement session on Mission Day 6, sharing imagery of the lunar far side.
Recovery forces, including the USS San Diego, are pre-positioning in the Pacific Ocean for the April 11 splashdown.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and following microgravity mitigation protocols.
history.lastEvent: Successful completion of lunar flyby and entry into the free return trajectory.
history.nextEvent: Separation of the Service Module followed by atmospheric re-entry at 40,000 km/h.

history.currentPhase: Orion is on the return coast to Earth following its historic lunar flyby.
history.headline: Artemis II crew breaks human distance record and begins Earth-bound trajectory corrections.
details: The crew reached a record distance of 406,771 km from Earth, surpassing the Apollo 13 record.
First return trajectory correction burn (RTC-1) successfully completed to fine-tune the reentry path.
Historic radio linkup performed between the Orion crew and the International Space Station.
Crew is evaluating orthostatic intolerance garments and monitoring a minor waste system filter issue.
sources: Artemis II Flight Operations Daily Briefing
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent spirits and health while conducting return-leg scientific observations.
history.lastEvent: Completion of the first return trajectory correction burn (RTC-1) on Flight Day 7.
history.nextEvent: Manual piloting demonstration of the Orion spacecraft handling systems.

history.headline: Orion spacecraft begins its return journey to Earth after shattering distance records and completing lunar science objectives.
details: Successfully executed the first return trajectory correction burn (RTC-1) with a 15-second thruster fire.
The crew of Orion performed a historic long-distance call with the International Space Station while 230,000 miles apart.
Engineers are monitoring a clogged filter in the waste management system, though backup systems remain fully operational.
The spacecraft has exited the lunar sphere of influence and is now being pulled back toward Earth by gravity.
history.crewStatus: Crew is in excellent health; currently preparing for orthostatic garment testing and manual piloting demonstrations.
history.lastEvent: Return Trajectory Correction 1 (RTC-1) completed at 00:03 UTC on April 8.
history.nextEvent: Orthostatic intolerance garment testing and secondary manual piloting demonstrations.

history.headline: Artemis II crew completes trajectory burn as they approach final stages of return journey.
details: The spacecraft Integrity completed a 15-second Return Trajectory Correction burn to fine-tune its path for a Pacific splashdown.
Crew surpassed the all-time human distance record from Earth, reaching 252,756 miles during their lunar flyby.
A filter blockage has been identified in the Orion waste system; the crew is successfully utilizing backup bag-and-funnel procedures.
NASA and the USS John P. Murtha are preparing for a scheduled splashdown off the coast of San Diego on April 10.
history.crewStatus: Crew is in good health and evaluating orthostatic garments to aid blood circulation during reentry.
history.lastEvent: Successful completion of the Return Trajectory Correction 1 (RTC-1) burn.
history.nextEvent: Manual piloting demonstrations and atmospheric reentry preparation.

history.currentPhase: The crew is on a return trajectory toward Earth following a successful lunar flyby and is currently in the seventh day of the 10-day mission.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins return journey after setting a new record for human distance from Earth.
details: On April 6, the crew reached a record distance of 406,771 km from Earth, surpassing the Apollo 13 record.
NASA is investigating a waste management issue involving a suspected debris blockage in the urine venting system.
The crew successfully performed a 15-second Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn on April 8 at 01:30 UTC.
Splashdown is currently targeted for April 11, 2026, at 00:07 UTC off the coast of San Diego.
sources: Artemis II Flight Status Report April 8, 2026
history.alertMessage: Engineers are monitoring the spacecraft waste management system due to a venting blockage; the system remains functional while a debris-related cause is investigated.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in good health.
history.lastEvent: Completed the RTC-1 burn using Orion's reaction control thrusters to refine the return path to Earth.
history.nextEvent: Second manual piloting demonstration and entry interface preparations.

history.headline: The Orion spacecraft is officially Earthbound following a successful Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn.
details: Orion successfully performed a 15-second RTC-1 burn to refine the entry corridor for splashdown.
The crew is preparing for the final phase of the mission by testing orthostatic intolerance garments.
The spacecraft exited the lunar sphere of influence on April 7, shifting focus toward Earth recovery.
Recovery operations are underway with the USS John P. Murtha transiting to the Pacific splashdown site.
history.crewStatus: The crew of four is in excellent health and high spirits as they transition toward re-entry preparation.
history.lastEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn completed at 01:03 UTC on April 8.

history.headline: Orion exits lunar sphere of influence following record-breaking mission objectives.
details: Orion officially departed the Moon's gravitational influence at 17:23 UTC, beginning its high-speed return to Earth.
The crew set a new human spaceflight distance record of 252,756 miles (406,771 km) from Earth, surpassing Apollo 13.
A historic 'moonship-to-spaceship' radio call was conducted between the Orion crew and the International Space Station.
A minor waste management system filter clog was reported; the crew is successfully using a backup bag-and-funnel system.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.

history.currentPhase: Orion is on a trans-Earth trajectory after exiting the lunar sphere of influence and is now accelerating toward splashdown.
history.headline: Orion spacecraft begins return journey to Earth after shattering human distance records.
details: Orion successfully completed its lunar flyby, passing within 4,067 miles of the surface and reaching a record distance of 252,756 miles from Earth.
The crew transitioned back to Earth's gravitational influence today at 17:23 UTC after exiting the lunar sphere of influence.
Medical data shows the crew is healthy and adapting well, despite expected minor shifts in sleep patterns and cardiovascular data.
A return trajectory correction maneuver (RTC-1) is scheduled for April 8 at 01:03 UTC to refine the re-entry path.
history.crewStatus: Crew is in excellent spirits; successfully completed an audio call with the ISS and designated a lunar feature 'Carroll' in a memorial gesture.
history.lastEvent: Exited the Lunar Sphere of Influence (SOI) at 17:23 UTC on April 7, 2026.
history.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) to refine the flight path for Pacific Ocean splashdown.

history.currentPhase: Orion has exited the lunar sphere of influence and is accelerating back toward Earth's gravity well.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins return journey after reaching record human distance during lunar flyby.
details: Orion reached a maximum human distance record of 252,756 miles (406,771 km) from Earth on April 6.
The crew completed a lunar flyby within 4,067 miles of the surface, witnessing a 57-minute deep space solar eclipse.
Spacecraft Integrity transitioned back to Earth's gravitational dominance at 17:25 UTC on April 7.
First Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) burn is scheduled for early April 8 to refine the splashdown path.
history.crewStatus: The crew—Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen—are in excellent health and are currently in a rest period before final mission operations.
history.lastEvent: Exit from the lunar sphere of influence at 17:25 UTC on April 7, 2026.
history.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction Burn (RTC-1) to refine the Earth reentry trajectory.

history.headline: Artemis II begins return journey after setting new human spaceflight distance record.
details: Orion has successfully exited the Moon's sphere of influence and is traveling back toward Earth.
The crew set a new human distance record of 406,771 km from Earth, surpassing Apollo 13.
Astronauts observed a total solar eclipse while passing behind the Moon during their closest approach.
Mission teams are preparing for return trajectory correction burns ahead of Friday's splashdown.
history.crewStatus: The crew—Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen—are in good health and conducting scientific debriefs.
history.lastEvent: Exit from the lunar sphere of influence.
history.nextEvent: Return trajectory correction burns to refine Earth entry course.

history.headline: Orion begins its journey back to Earth after setting a new record for the farthest distance traveled by humans in deep space.
details: Orion successfully exited the lunar sphere of influence at 17:23 UTC, returning to Earth's gravitational dominance.
The crew set a new all-time human distance record of 406,771 km (252,756 miles) from Earth on April 6.
Orion achieved its closest lunar approach of 6,545 km (4,067 miles) above the surface during its flyby.
Crew members Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen remain in excellent health following a 53-minute solar eclipse observation.
history.crewStatus: The four-person crew is in excellent health and high spirits, currently focused on scientific data entry and preparing for return burns.
history.lastEvent: Exit from the lunar sphere of influence on April 7 at 17:23 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Return Trajectory Correction (RTC-1) thruster ignition to refine the path toward the San Diego splashdown zone.

history.currentPhase: Homeward bound return trajectory following lunar flyby
history.headline: Artemis II Orion begins Earth return after breaking human distance records during lunar flyby.
details: Orion exited the lunar sphere of influence at 17:25 UTC on April 7, 2026, and is now falling back toward Earth's gravity.
The mission achieved a record-breaking maximum distance from Earth of 252,756 miles (406,771 km) on April 6.
A first return trajectory correction burn (RTC-1) was successfully executed on April 7 at 00:03 UTC.
The crew remains in excellent health and reported being 'euphoric' following solar eclipse observations from the lunar far side.
history.crewStatus: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent spirits and transition to return-flight procedures.
history.lastEvent: Exited the lunar sphere of influence at 17:25 UTC on April 7, 2026.
history.nextEvent: Second return trajectory correction burn (RTC-2) to fine-tune the atmospheric reentry angle.

history.currentPhase: Orion is currently on a free-return trajectory coasting back to Earth after completing its lunar flyby.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins return journey to Earth after setting a new human distance record in space.
details: Orion reached a maximum distance of 252,756 miles from Earth, surpassing the record set by Apollo 13.
The crew documented a solar eclipse and observed four meteoroid impact flashes while on the lunar far side.
NASA flight surgeons report the crew is in excellent health with radiation exposure well within safe mission limits.
The spacecraft is scheduled to exit the Lunar Sphere of Influence at 17:23 UTC followed by a trajectory correction burn.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and maintaining high performance despite minor sleep reduction.
history.lastEvent: Completion of record-breaking lunar flyby and far-side science observations.
history.nextEvent: Exit from the Lunar Sphere of Influence followed by RTC-1 maneuver.

history.currentPhase: The Orion spacecraft is on a free-return trajectory heading back to Earth.
history.headline: Artemis II crew begins return transit following record-breaking lunar flyby.
details: Orion reached a maximum distance from Earth of 252,756 miles (406,771 km), a new record for human spaceflight.
The crew successfully resolved a minor frozen toilet vent by rotating the spacecraft for a sunlight-induced 'sun-soak'.
Astronauts observed a 57-minute solar eclipse while positioned behind the Moon during their homeward slingshot.
The crew proposed naming two lunar craters 'Integrity' and 'Carroll' during an emotional downlink with Mission Control.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and performing biomedical monitoring.
history.lastEvent: The crew completed a 57-minute solar eclipse observation while behind the Moon.
history.nextEvent: Orion will exit the lunar sphere of influence and transition back to Earth-dominant gravity.
